text.title: Reviewer roles and attack vectors
text.summary: Phase 3 launches exactly 8 reviewer subagents, each assigned one attack vector from the required list. One vector per reviewer prevents the "polite generalist" failure mode. Optional roles may be added when the domain warrants. Every reviewer prompt is adversarial: find what is wrong, not what is right.

rule.statement: Each reviewer MUST be assigned exactly one attack vector and explicitly told to ignore the other vectors.
rule.rationale: A reviewer asked to evaluate everything ends up evaluating nothing — the prompt becomes "is this generally good?" which is rubber-stamping. A reviewer told to attack only feasibility produces a sharp, useful critique on feasibility. Coverage comes from running 8 single-vector reviewers in parallel, not from one reviewer covering 8 vectors.

rule.statement: Reviewer prompts MUST be adversarial in framing: "find what breaks", "find hidden costs", "find missing perspectives" — never "review and confirm".
rule.rationale: Adversarial framing produces actionable critique. Confirmatory framing produces "LGTM" comments that the synthesis pass cannot use to re-tier.

list.item: Feasibility & Effort — looks for: unrealistic estimates, hidden dependencies, ordering violations, work that requires upstream changes the plan does not account for.
Security — looks for: gaps introduced by recommendations, missing minimum-viable hardening (authn, authz, secrets, sec headers, audit log), threat-model coverage.
Architecture — looks for: coherence of the recommendation set, contradictions between recs, ordering that creates throwaway work, layering violations.
Solo Dev / DX — looks for: maintenance burden per rec, toil added vs removed, on-call impact, debug time cost, paved-path violations.
Cost-Benefit — looks for: actual dollar impact (cloud, vendor, time), ROI rank, hidden costs (training, migration, vendor lock), recommendations whose payback is >12 months.
Missing Perspectives — looks for: what the diverge phase missed (user-facing features if the recs are infra-heavy, data/ETL if the recs are app-only, operations if the recs are dev-only).
Domain Expert — looks for: deep-dive on the top-ranked recommendation, surfaces domain-specific risks the generalists missed, validates the proposed mechanism.
Prioritization Synthesis — looks for: final ordering with all reviewer feedback integrated, explicit tier moves with rationale, devil's-advocate items the user should still see.

section.title: Optional reviewer roles
section.p: Add when the domain warrants. Each optional role still gets exactly one attack vector. Total reviewer count may rise to 10, never above.

list.item: Accessibility — looks for: WCAG violations introduced, keyboard / screen-reader regressions.
Regulatory / compliance — looks for: GDPR / HIPAA / PCI exposure, data residency, retention policy violations.
Performance — looks for: latency budget violations, N+1 patterns, payload-size regressions.
Internationalization — looks for: hardcoded strings, locale-specific formatting bugs, RTL breaks.
Observability — looks for: metric / log / trace gaps the recommendations leave behind.

rule.statement: Every reviewer MUST emit a verdict per recommendation: KEEP / DEMOTE / DROP, with one-line rationale and a tier suggestion if KEEP.
rule.rationale: The Phase 4 re-tiering pass needs structured input. Free-form prose cannot drive the tier moves; per-rec verdicts can.
